caveats: a: The available BLS occupation table is NAICS 455000 rather than 455110 and includes warehouse clubs and supercenters.
a: Macy's is a large-company implementation example and may have capabilities unavailable to LMM operators.
a: The estimate excludes opportunity already automated and does not treat physical robotics as AI substitution unless it avoids labor within five years.
rho: Large-chain adoption can overstate LMM implementation capacity.
rho: The range assumes vendor tools mature and integrate with common POS, ERP, e-commerce, and workforce systems.
rho: This is implementation of exposed labor opportunity, not technology availability or gross productivity potential.
e: The injected firm-count input is missing, so this share cannot be reconciled to a defensible eligible-firm count.
e: NAICS assignment is establishment-based and can obscure multi-format retailers and parent-company ownership.
e: Eligibility is estimated rather than directly observed.
s5: Public sales data show sector pressure but do not measure private-company control transfers.
s5: Distress can produce liquidation or lease termination rather than a transferable operating company.
s5: No defensible industry-specific denominator or closed-deal series was found.
q: There are few recurring customer contracts, so renewal repricing is indirect through ongoing price competition.
q: Margin retention varies sharply by private-label penetration, local differentiation, and product overlap.
q: The estimate is a discounted five-year share of implemented gross benefit, not gross margin.
d5: The sales series is nominal and includes large chains, while the primitive is constant-price and specific to the LMM lens.
d5: Pandemic years distort comparisons and format reclassification may affect the series.
d5: Merchandise sales are only a proxy for the quantity of the retail service basket.
o: The boundary between software-enabled retailing and software replacing the retailer is judgmental.
o: Macy's digital sales share is a large-chain proxy and digital purchases can still require the same accountable operator.
o: Volume loss is reserved for d5; this primitive only addresses who supplies surviving quantity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly consumer merchandise sales at posted or promoted prices, with repeat but usually non-contractual purchases; ancillary delivery, licensed-department, marketplace, media, and credit-card-related income may supplement merchandise margin.
lens.excluded_activities: Public and very large national chains outside the size band, non-operating real-estate or financing entities, captive internal units, shells, and stores whose economics cannot be transferred apart from a parent chain.
lens.included_activities: US department-store operators in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly retail broad lines of merchandise and associated shopping, fulfillment, return, and customer-support services to external consumers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment shares for the broader NAICS 455000 general-merchandise population, not wage-weighted task exposure in 455110. I map customer service, merchandising, marketing, scheduling, checkout, inventory planning, and administrative portions of those occupations to AI-exposed tasks, while treating most physical selling, stocking, and handling as unexposed; the broader category's heavy supercenter mix likely overweights physical stocking and biases the estimate downward for department stores.
caveats: The available BLS occupation table is NAICS 455000 rather than 455110 and includes warehouse clubs and supercenters.
Macy's is a large-company implementation example and may have capabilities unavailable to LMM operators.
The estimate excludes opportunity already automated and does not treat physical robotics as AI substitution unless it avoids labor within five years.
a.change_evidence: A 455110-specific payroll-weighted task inventory from several LMM operators, including time studies for associates, buyers, marketers, customer support, and replenishment staff, plus baseline automation already deployed.
a.rationale: The adjacent-industry occupation mix is dominated by sales and material-moving work: sales occupations are 43.63% and transportation/material-moving occupations 33.00% of employment, while office support is 8.41%. AI can assist service, personalization, planning, marketing, HR queries, coding, and supply-chain decisions, but most floor presence and item handling remain physical. Macy's confirms active AI use across many of those functions.

d5.bridge: The Census/FRED series measures nominal sales for all US department stores, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity for LMM operators. I use the decline from roughly $3.6-$4.0 billion per month during much of 2021-22 to roughly $3.2-$3.4 billion in 2025 as a directional baseline, then allow for inflation and LMM/local differentiation; nominal sales likely overstate real service-basket demand, biasing an unadjusted ratio upward.
caveats: The sales series is nominal and includes large chains, while the primitive is constant-price and specific to the LMM lens.
Pandemic years distort comparisons and format reclassification may affect the series.
Merchandise sales are only a proxy for the quantity of the retail service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 455110 sales and transaction counts by store format and ownership size, including closures and e-commerce fulfilled by stores, plus five-year cohort retention for independent operators.
d5.rationale: Department-store sales have fallen structurally and remained below the 2021-22 level through 2025, while e-commerce continues to grow faster than total retail. A further real decline over five years is therefore more likely than growth, although recent stabilization, differentiated local formats, and omnichannel fulfillment keep the downside bounded.

caveats: The injected firm-count input is missing, so this share cannot be reconciled to a defensible eligible-firm count.
NAICS assignment is establishment-based and can obscure multi-format retailers and parent-company ownership.
Eligibility is estimated rather than directly observed.
e.change_evidence: A business-register census for 455110 linking establishments to ultimate owners, normalized EBITDA, lease assignability, and standalone financial statements, followed by broker interviews on actual saleability.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is a broad-line, fixed-point-of-sale retail model, so many establishments are branches of chains rather than independently transferable firms. Among putative LMM firms, additional exclusions arise from owner dependence, weak leases, inadequate systems, distressed inventory, or economics embedded in a buying group or parent. A minority should still be independent, repeat-customer operators with separable operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The boundary between software-enabled retailing and software replacing the retailer is judgmental.
Macy's digital sales share is a large-chain proxy and digital purchases can still require the same accountable operator.
Volume loss is reserved for d5; this primitive only addresses who supplies surviving quantity.
o.change_evidence: Consumer journey and transaction data separating store-assisted, self-service, marketplace, direct-brand, and fully automated purchases, together with the share of fulfillment and returns requiring retailer accountability.
o.rationale: Most surviving department-store demand still requires an accountable merchant to select and finance inventory, manage vendors, operate stores and digital channels, handle returns, and stand behind the transaction. Self-checkout, agentic shopping, marketplaces, and direct-to-consumer channels can remove pieces of work or bypass a store, but they do not eliminate the operator role for most remaining department-store volume.

caveats: There are few recurring customer contracts, so renewal repricing is indirect through ongoing price competition.
Margin retention varies sharply by private-label penetration, local differentiation, and product overlap.
The estimate is a discounted five-year share of implemented gross benefit, not gross margin.
q.change_evidence: Store-level contribution-margin cohorts for operators that deployed AI, tracking realized labor savings, price and promotion changes, customer-service reinvestment, traffic, and competitor response through at least two annual merchandise cycles.
q.rationale: Retail pricing is not cost-plus, so an operator initially retains labor savings rather than contractually passing them through. Retention is nevertheless eroded by promotional pricing, rapid competitor imitation, service reinvestment, vendor negotiations, and customers' ability to compare identical goods across stores and online. Macy's explicitly identifies price, service, location, assortment, and online channels as competitive dimensions.

caveats: Large-chain adoption can overstate LMM implementation capacity.
The range assumes vendor tools mature and integrate with common POS, ERP, e-commerce, and workforce systems.
This is implementation of exposed labor opportunity, not technology availability or gross productivity potential.
rho.change_evidence: Matched before-and-after labor-hour and error-rate data from 10-20 LMM department-store deployments, split by workflow and including failed or rolled-back implementations.
rho.rationale: Half of exposed opportunity is a reasonable five-year implementation case: packaged tools should diffuse in marketing, service, workforce administration, and planning, but legacy systems, small-firm data quality, integration cost, privacy, bias, inventory errors, and the need for human review constrain deployment. Macy's describes both broad operating uses and explicit governance and implementation risks.

caveats: Public sales data show sector pressure but do not measure private-company control transfers.
Distress can produce liquidation or lease termination rather than a transferable operating company.
No defensible industry-specific denominator or closed-deal series was found.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-transaction records from retail-focused brokers, Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, and SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, filtered to independent 455110 going concerns and matched to the eligible denominator.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer rate in the mid-teens allows for retirement, distress, strategic consolidation, and ordinary owner exits but discounts closures, liquidations, internal reorganizations, and family succession without a qualifying control transfer. Department-store pressure can create events while simultaneously reducing the share that remain transferable going concerns.

principal_driver: Retention of workflow savings in a posted-price model across service, merchandising, planning, and administrative tasks.
principal_weakness: Structural demand erosion and a poorly observed, likely small pool of independently transferable LMM operators.

caveats: S1 supports the industry definition but not the asserted prevalence of chain branches or the 0.25 eligible-firm-share estimate; e is nevertheless honestly labeled ESTIMATE.

The a bridge starts from employment shares, not wage-weighted task exposure, and provides no explicit occupational wage or task calculation for the 0.29 base; the broader-industry population and likely error direction are disclosed.
S4 demonstrates AI use and implementation risks at Macy's but cannot establish LMM adoption, exposure, retention, or operator-necessity magnitudes for a, rho, q, or o.
The 0.15 s5 transfer-rate estimate has no direct transaction evidence; S5 supplies demand and distress context only, a limitation the record explicitly acknowledges.
The d5 range extrapolates a historical nominal, all-chain sales series and total-retail e-commerce growth into forward constant-price LMM quantity without an explicit inflation or cohort calculation.
The q range is entirely above the 0.50 factor cap, fixing C at 10 despite the cited report documenting intense price and channel competition and providing no realized savings-retention evidence.

The 0.88 o estimate is not directly measured by S4 or S6; those sources establish digital-channel importance and retail e-commerce growth, not the share of surviving quantity that still requires a department-store operator.

sources_audited.note: The BLS page supports the 455110 definition and fixed-point-of-sale, broad-line retail model, but it does not establish chain ownership prevalence or the estimated eligible-firm share.
The May 2023 OEWS table is explicitly for NAICS 455000 and reports the cited 43.63% sales, 33.00% transportation/material-moving, and 8.41% office-support employment shares.

Macy's 2025 annual report supports the stated AI use cases, governance and legacy-system risks, competitive dimensions, fulfillment and returns context, and 35% digital-sales share, but it is a large-chain example and does not directly measure LMM primitive values.

The Census-sourced FRED series is monthly, seasonally adjusted, nominal department-store sales and supports the cited decline from much of 2021-22 to 2025; it does not measure private-company control transfers or forward constant-price LMM demand.

The Census page reports the cited first-quarter 2026 e-commerce growth, total-retail growth, and 16.9% share, but the data are economy-wide retail measures rather than department-store-specific evidence for d5 or o.

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 455110 as department stores with separate departments for broad lines of new merchandise and fixed point-of-sale locations; informs business-model and eligibility analysis.
sources.title: PPI Coverage of the Retail Trade Sector

sources.supports: Reports the adjacent-industry occupation mix, including 43.63% sales, 8.41% office support, and 33.00% transportation/material-moving employment; supports the task-exposure proxy.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 455000 General Merchandise Retailers

sources.supports: Projects cashier employment to decline 10% from 2024 to 2034 and attributes the decline partly to self-service checkout and online sales; supports automation and substitution context.
sources.title: Cashiers: Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: Documents AI use in service, personalization, marketing, HR, coding, supply chain, merchandising and planning; identifies governance and legacy-system risks, price and online competition, and a 35% digital-sales share in 2025.
sources.title: Macy's, Inc. 2025 Annual Report

sources.supports: Census-sourced monthly nominal sales series showing roughly $3.6-$4.0 billion monthly in much of 2021-22 versus roughly $3.2-$3.4 billion in 2025; supports the demand-direction proxy and distress context.
sources.title: Retail Sales: Department Stores, Seasonally Adjusted

sources.supports: Reports US retail e-commerce sales up 9.8% year over year in first-quarter 2026 versus 3.9% for total retail, with e-commerce at 16.9% of total sales; supports channel-substitution context.
sources.title: Quarterly Retail E-Commerce Sales, First Quarter 2026

title: Department Stores
